questions.question: Explain the difference between shallow learning and deep learning with respect to feature engineering.
keyPoints: Shallow learning requires manual feature engineering by humans
Deep learning automatically learns hierarchical features from raw data
Deep learning progressively extracts higher-level features from lower-level raw inputs
Shallow networks typically have 1-2 hidden layers

questions.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem? How does it affect deep neural network training?
keyPoints: Gradients become extremely small during backpropagation
Caused by activation functions like sigmoid and tanh with derivatives less than 1
Weights receive minimal updates, learning slows drastically
Makes it difficult to train networks with many layers
ReLU and batch normalization help mitigate this problem

questions.question: Describe backpropagation and its role in training neural networks.
keyPoints: Algorithm for computing gradients of loss with respect to weights
Uses chain rule to propagate error signals backward from output to input
Computes gradient at each layer by combining local gradient with upstream gradient
Weights are updated in opposite direction of gradient to minimize loss
Enables networks to learn from errors

questions.question: What is overfitting in deep learning? Mention two techniques to prevent it.
keyPoints: Model learns training data too well including noise
Fails to generalize to unseen data
High training accuracy but poor test accuracy
Techniques: Dropout, L1/L2 Regularization, Early stopping, Data augmentation, Batch normalization

questions.question: Explain the concept of learning versus pure optimization in deep learning.
keyPoints: Pure optimization minimizes loss function on training data
Learning aims to generalize to unseen data beyond training set
Optimization focuses on finding minimum of loss surface
Learning requires preventing overfitting and ensuring generalization
Regularization techniques distinguish learning from pure optimization

questions.question: What are activation functions? Why are non-linear activation functions necessary?
keyPoints: Functions applied to weighted sum of inputs to produce output
Introduce non-linearity into the network
Without non-linearity, multiple layers collapse into single linear transformation
Common functions: Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU
Enable networks to learn complex patterns

questions.question: List three challenges in neural network optimization and briefly explain each.
keyPoints: Vanishing gradients: gradients become too small to learn
Exploding gradients: gradients become too large causing instability
Local minima: network gets stuck in suboptimal solutions
Plateaus: flat regions where learning slows significantly
Choice of learning rate: too high causes divergence, too low slows learning

questions.question: Explain the architecture of a deep neural network. How does it learn hierarchical feature representations? Discuss with an example.
keyPoints: Deep networks have multiple hidden layers between input and output
Each layer transforms input into increasingly abstract representations
Lower layers learn basic features (edges, textures)
Higher layers combine basic features into complex concepts (objects, faces)
Backpropagation enables end-to-end learning from raw data
Example: Image classification—pixels → edges → textures → parts → objects → categories

questions.question: Discuss the challenges faced in training deep neural networks. Explain how techniques like dropout, batch normalization, and proper weight initialization address these challenges.
keyPoints: Overfitting: dropout randomly deactivates neurons forcing robust learning
Internal covariate shift: batch normalization normalizes layer inputs
Gradient problems: proper initialization (Xavier, He) and ReLU activations
Learning rate selection: adaptive optimizers like Adam help
Training time: GPU acceleration, efficient architectures
Explaining how each technique specifically addresses each challenge

questions.question: Compare gradient descent, stochastic gradient descent, and mini-batch gradient descent. Discuss their advantages and disadvantages in the context of deep learning training.
keyPoints: Batch GD: uses entire dataset per update, smooth convergence, slow for large datasets
SGD: uses one sample per update, noisy but fast, can escape local minima
Mini-batch GD: uses subset of data, balances speed and convergence quality
Trade-offs: convergence speed, noise, computational efficiency
Mini-batch is standard in deep learning due to GPU efficiency and good convergence
Size of mini-batch (32-512) affects both training speed and generalization

questions.question: Explain the concept of loss functions in deep learning. How does the choice of loss function affect training? Provide examples for different types of problems.
keyPoints: Loss function measures difference between predicted and actual outputs
Cross-entropy loss for classification problems
Mean Squared Error (MSE) for regression problems
Hinge loss for SVM-style classification
Choice affects gradient landscape and learning dynamics
Appropriate loss function aligned with problem type is crucial for effective training
Example: binary cross-entropy for binary classification, categorical cross-entropy for multi-class
